selectedOption: Adopt an owner-only, disabled-by-default feature-flagged Demo Clone MVP with an allowlist converter, same-device and same-browser recovery through a minimal versioned localStorage journal, and cleaned text-only places explicitly labelled as unverified. Technical development is Emulator-only after Human Approval of this Decision; production enablement requires separate approval of Firebase Auth, ownership and membership, Database and Storage Rules, migration compatibility, rule tests, rollout, and rollback.
rationale: The Clone CTA is a distinct, explicit operation available only inside Built-in Demo Preview. It is absent from the Lobby entry card, Settings, real TripDetail, and Offline Preview; Open Demo, Blank Create, and Clone remain three different actions.

The owner-only MVP avoids converting fictional display members into authorization identities. The versioned three-day itinerary is copied, checklist completion is reset, and checklist assignees are either unassigned or the current owner; real collaborators wait for a separately approved invitation and membership model.

Tickets, expenses, settlement data, attachments, Storage paths, Demo IDs, Demo-only root fields, guidance, example URLs, DEMO order numbers, historical timestamps, audit or completion state, and credential-like data are excluded because they are unnecessary for a safe itinerary template and may carry stale, sensitive, or identity-bound state.

An allowlist converter constructs a fresh object graph instead of mutating or deep-copying the Demo. It creates a new room ID and regenerates place and checklist IDs; retry-stable IDs derive deterministically from a stable operation ID and template source key. The Demo room ID can never become the new trip ID, and the Demo never enters myTrips or Offline Cache.

Cleaned text-only places are explicitly labelled as unverified because copied Demo text, coordinates, place IDs, and routing metadata are not proof of a verified navigable location. The MVP copies no trusted place_id, and users may later reselect a place through Google Places.

The versioned localStorage journal promises only same-device, same-browser recovery because browser-local state cannot provide cross-device idempotency. It stores only schemaVersion, operationId, roomId, templateVersion, title, startDate, payloadFingerprint, state, createdAt, and updatedAt—not the transformed trip payload.

The journal is untrusted input. Recovery revalidates its schema, template version, identifiers, and fingerprint; availability is checked by write/read/remove before confirmation; unavailable storage blocks confirmation; retention is seven days; expired or incompatible entries never write automatically; and the fingerprint is only a consistency check, not authorization or a signature.

Technical development and production enablement are separate gates so the pure converter, confirmation UI, operation state machine, local journal, and Emulator integration can be validated behind a disabled-by-default flag without implying that production identity or authorization is approved.

The technical MVP assembles the complete payload before an Emulator-only create-only transaction, or equivalent semantics, writes an operation marker and payload fingerprint, and resolves timeout or ambiguous outcomes by read-back without automatically deleting a possibly successful room.

After the Firebase room is verified, myTrips must be written synchronously and read back before navigation to real TripDetail. A local-reference failure retains the journal and exposes repair or open-link state so a successful room is not lost or falsely reported as fully linked.

Confirm double-clicks share one in-flight operation; retry and refresh reuse the operationId, roomId, and fingerprint; the same successful operation remains successful; a room collision with a different operation or fingerprint is rejected; and only a newly completed Wizard may intentionally create another trip.

Production remains disabled until separate approval covers Firebase Auth identity, owner/member/invitation semantics, Database Rules, Storage Rules, legacy-room migration and compatibility, production rule tests, and rollout and rollback plans. This Proposal does not approve Firebase Rules changes, production enablement, or deployment.
acceptedArguments: Round 1 correctly requires an explicit Demo-only Clone action, a fresh allowlist-built object graph, strict exclusion of Demo-only and identity-bound data, whole-room Firebase creation, ambiguous-outcome read-back, Demo immutability, and separation from Offline Preview.

Round 1 correctly identifies stable operation identity, deterministic derived IDs, operation fingerprints, and ordered Firebase then myTrips verification as necessary duplicate and partial-failure controls.

Round 2 correctly narrows the first release to an owner-only trip, separates technical development from production authorization, and rejects any claim that a device-local journal provides general or cross-device idempotency.

Round 2 correctly requires a minimal journal data contract, seven-day retention and compatibility handling, untrusted-input validation, and a clear distinction between fingerprint consistency and authorization.

Round 2 correctly requires a product contract for copied place data; this Proposal selects cleaned text-only entries labelled as unverified while leaving a future production requirement for mandatory Google Places reselection open.
Both rounds support keeping the Built-in Demo local-only, read-only, outside myTrips and Offline Cache writes, and distinct from the newly created real trip.
rejectedAlternatives: Deep-copying or mutating the Demo, because it can carry forbidden identifiers, stale state, and Demo-only fields into a real trip.
Reusing the Blank Create Modal as Clone, because Open Demo, Blank Create, and Clone require distinct consent, transformation, and recovery semantics.
Mapping all three fictional Demo members in the first release, because display labels are not authenticated authorization identities.
Claiming localStorage supplies cross-device idempotency, because its state is limited to one browser or device and may be cleared or unavailable.

Copying tickets, expenses, settlements, or attachments in the first release, because these objects carry higher identity, privacy, storage, and reconciliation risk and are not required for an itinerary-template MVP.
Presenting copied Demo place data as a verified location without an unverified label, because Demo text and coordinates are not trusted Google Places verification.
Enabling production Clone before Firebase Auth, ownership, membership, invitations, Database Rules, and Storage Rules are approved and tested.
risks: The localStorage journal may be lost, expired, unavailable, stale, or tampered with.
A Firebase timeout or interrupted response may leave an ambiguously successful room.
The Firebase room may succeed while the myTrips reference write or verification fails.
Multiple tabs or rapid confirmation may start duplicate operations.
Text-only unverified places may mislead users about navigation accuracy.
Adding Clone orchestration to App.jsx may increase coupling and state-management complexity.
A disabled-by-default feature flag may be accidentally enabled in an unauthorized environment.
The current broad time-based Database and Storage Rules do not establish authenticated owner or member authorization.
mitigations: For journal loss or tampering, store only minimal versioned metadata, run write/read/remove availability preflight, apply a seven-day expiry, treat every record as untrusted, and reject invalid versions, IDs, states, or fingerprints without automatic writes.

For ambiguous Firebase success, use create-only semantics with an operation marker and fingerprint, read back the expected room, accept only the matching operation, and never auto-delete on ambiguity.
For myTrips failure, verify the local reference synchronously before navigation, retain the journal, and expose repair or open-link recovery for an already verified room.

For multi-tab or rapid duplicates, share one in-flight operation in the active flow and use stable operationId, roomId, deterministic derived IDs, and fingerprint collision checks across retry and reload.
For place accuracy, strip trusted place_id and routing claims, label copied entries as unverified text-only places, and allow later Google Places reselection.
For App.jsx complexity, isolate the pure converter, journal adapter, and operation state machine behind narrow interfaces and test them independently before UI integration.
For accidental flag enablement, default the feature flag to disabled, verify the disabled state in regression tests, and require a separately reviewed production configuration and rollout plan.

For broad current Rules, restrict technical work to Firebase Emulator and block production enablement until Auth, ownership, Database Rules, Storage Rules, and production rule tests receive separate approval.
requiredTests: Pure allowlist converter unit tests covering the complete versioned three-day itinerary and reset checklist.

Denylist serialization tests proving tickets, expenses, settlements, attachments, Storage paths, Demo IDs, Demo-only fields, guidance, example URLs, DEMO order numbers, old timestamps, audit state, and credential-like data are absent.
Deterministic ID tests for operationId, roomId, place IDs, and checklist IDs across retry and reload, including collision rejection.
Date boundary tests across month, year, daylight-saving, locale, and invalid start-date cases.
Owner-only member tests proving fictional members are excluded and checklist assignees become unassigned or the current owner.
Journal schema, minimal-field, availability, seven-day expiry, version incompatibility, migration rejection, and tampering tests.
Operation state-machine tests for confirmation, Firebase write, read-back, myTrips write, verification, repair, success, and terminal failure states.
Double-click, multi-tab, retry, and reload tests proving one operation is reused and a newly completed Wizard can intentionally create another trip.
Ambiguous Firebase timeout and read-back tests proving matching success is recovered and mismatched operation or fingerprint is rejected without room deletion.
myTrips failure and repair tests proving navigation waits for verification and the journal remains available for recovery.
Demo immutability tests proving Clone never mutates Demo data or uses its room ID.
Offline Preview isolation tests proving neither the Demo nor Clone source writes to Offline Cache and Offline Preview exposes no Clone CTA.
Database and Storage Emulator tests for create-only behavior, ownership boundaries, forbidden writes, and attachment exclusion before any production gate.
Desktop Chrome and Mobile Safari Playwright coverage for CTA placement, confirmation, failure recovery, retry, and post-success navigation, to run only in the later authorized implementation phase.
Feature-flag-disabled regression tests proving Clone is unavailable by default and no Firebase, myTrips, or Offline Cache writes occur.
unresolvedQuestions: What authenticated owner, member, and invitation authorization model will govern production rooms and collaboration?
Will a future release require cross-device idempotency through a trusted backend per-UID operation ledger?
Must production require every cloned place to be reselected through Google Places instead of retaining labelled unverified text-only entries?
What migration and compatibility policy applies to legacy rooms when production ownership and Rules are introduced?

evidence.claim: Round 1 defines the explicit Demo-only Clone boundary, allowlist conversion, operation identity, Firebase read-back, and ordered myTrips verification.
Round 2 narrows the MVP to owner-only, separates development and production gates, limits the journal guarantee, and requires an explicit place-data contract.
The current application coordinates blank-trip creation, rooms writes, myTrips references, and navigation.
The Demo fixture contains fictional members, itinerary data, checklist data, budgets, and example-only values that require allowlist conversion.
The Built-in Demo Preview is the bounded UI surface selected for the explicit Clone CTA.
Offline Preview uses a separate read-only cache path that must remain outside Demo Clone writes.
Current Database Rules are broad time-based grants and do not define authenticated owner or member authorization.
Current Storage Rules require separate review before any attachment-capable production flow.
The project risk register distinguishes display or device identity from authorization and requires Auth and Rules review.
evidence.symbol: R-H01 Device identity mistaken for authorization
